reviews.author: Daniel Vacek
reviews.summary: reviewer argued that csums can be returned for compressed and encrypted extents, even if the permission check fails, by flagging them as ENCODED
reviews.raw_body:
I'd say there is no reason not to return the csums. Even for
compressed or encrypted extents, you can flag them ENCODED and still
return the csum (given the permission check). Userspace is free to
ignore it.

Note that you can read/write the raw data with the
BTRFS_IOC_ENCODED_{READ|WRITE} ioctl.

Other than that it looks like a very nice speed-up.

--nX

reviews.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged Daniel's feedback that userspace might not want a copy of csums, agreed to remove this feature for now and consider adding it as a later flag.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks Daniel. My concern there is that this probably won't be what 
userspace wants, so you'll be doing a copy for no reason. I'll think 
I'll just keep it for a later flag, so we can add this later if and when 
we need it.

One thing I'm working on at the moment is "encoded reflinks", so you can 
mark uncompressed data as being part of a compressed extent or vice 
versa. If we extended this to encryption, this would be a use case for 
this ioctl: you would be able to do mkfs.btrfs --rootdir --reflink on an 
encrypted directory without needing to have loaded the key.

2026-04-13.patch_summary: This patch adds an ioctl (input/output control) command to the Btrfs file system called BTRFS_IOC_GET_CSUMS, which allows users to retrieve checksums of files. The main reason for this is to prevent potential security vulnerabilities by not exposing arbitrary checksums to unprivileged users. However, it also provides a useful feature for userspace applications that can XOR multiple checksums together for ad-hoc checksum verification.
